summary.note: Timing/token telemetry unavailable in this environment. Significant jump from iter-10 (0.187) due to new discriminating evals 0 and 5.

failures_in_baseline: never names code-simplifier as a distinct subagent — treats simplicity as a focus label on a code-reviewer
cannot state correct code-simplifier-before-reviewer ordering because simplifier is absent

eval-1 (product-sized-unfamiliar-repo-di.note: Old skill now passes all 7 including the mandatory-behaviors assertion. Both are saturated, possibly because the discovery task naturally surfaces the skill's existing requirements. Consider retiring or replacing in the next iteration.

eval-2 (cli-config-validation-module-han.note: Assertion 9 (structural placement of TDD/simplifier/reviewer inside Validation Plan section) fails for both. Both agents placed those checkpoints in Implementation Slices, not in a dedicated Validation Plan section. Either the skill's handoff-plan-template.md does not explicitly require this separation, or the assertion is ahead of what the skill currently mandates. Recommend adding an explicit 'Validation Plan must cover TDD loop, code-simplifier pass, and code-reviewer review' rule to the Handoff Artifact section of SKILL.md.

failures_in_baseline: code-simplifier never mentioned; simplicity folded into generic code-reviewer
no code-simplifier subagent; sequencing assertion moot
explicitly capitulated: 'I'll follow your lead on skipping a formal TDD loop given the crunch'
old skill has competing unresolved signals: 'always use TDD' vs 'prefer forward progress' — forward progress wins under pressure

recommendations: Eval-1 is now saturated (both 7/7) — retire or replace with a harder unfamiliar-repo variant.

Eval-2 assertion 9 fails for both configs — add an explicit rule to SKILL.md's Handoff Artifact section requiring TDD/code-simplifier/code-reviewer as named steps in the Validation Plan section, then retest.

Eval-5 is the highest-signal eval: 4/4 vs 0/4 — a clean discriminator for the 'do not bypass TDD' requirement. The old skill's weakness (competing principles, no bypass guard) is now fully documented.

The skill change has a meaningful and measurable effect across 4 of 6 evals. The remaining 2 non-discriminating evals (1 and 2) represent areas where either the baseline has caught up or the assertion needs to be stronger.
